21 EXT./INT. TENT CITY/ THEATER - NIGHT
CAMERA swoops over the occupants of the tent city parking lot, toward
the remains of an abandoned movie tpeater that rests on the property.
The theater windows are boarded up. The marquee is shattered. CAMERA
GOES ins_ide, past the empty lobby, into the theater.
The remaining seats ~re torn and broken. The floor is strewn with old
coffee cups, candy wrappers and garbage. An occasional rodent scurries
by. Years of dust and cobwebs have accumulated on the walls and remaining
fixtures. The screen is stained, ripped. Standing on the battered wooden
stage, is JOANNE JEFFERSON, an elegant black woman in her mid-twenties.
She's Harvard educated, intelligent, tough and anal retentive. She's
tinkering with a complicated sound system with one hand, a pay phone
receiver is cradled under her chin.

How do you measure, measure a year?
They say their goodbyes, embrace, parting.

The couples go their separate ways. Maureen and Joanner arm in arm,

They walk off down the street, toward the loft.
Mark stands alone on the street, Bolex camera under his arm. He watches
Roger and Mimi, a small smile appea!."s on his face-, tr,.ily happy for his
friend. But his expression offers a subtle sense of melancholy, of
loneliness. Mark responds by doing what he does best. He raises his
camera, points at Roger and Mimi, and films.
(SEASONS OF LOVE continues, illustrated with footage shot by Mark,
in 16MM and SUPER 8. The footage is grainy, home movie-like, shaky.)
